GRD.letter: Hello! Grenada smells amazing. Literally. It is the 'Spice Isle' and produces more nutmeg per person than anywhere on Earth. You can smell cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg in the AIR just walking around. The underwater sculpture park off the coast is one of the most unique art installations in the world: statues placed on the sea floor where coral grows on them over time. Grenada also produces some of the finest chocolate in the Caribbean. In 1983, the United States actually invaded Grenada in a military operation that lasted just a few days.

ATG.letter: Wah gwaan! Antigua has 365 beaches, one for every day of the year. The annual sailing regatta attracts boats from around the world. Nelson's Dockyard is the only continuously working Georgian dockyard in the world. The annual sailing regatta is one of the biggest in the Caribbean. Nelson's Dockyard was built in the 1700s and is STILL a working marina.

WSM.letter: Talofa! Samoa is where the Polynesian culture is strongest. Fa'a Samoa (the Samoan Way) governs daily life. Samoans are some of the best rugby players on Earth. To Sua Ocean Trench is a stunning natural swimming hole. Robert Louis Stevenson, who wrote Treasure Island, loved Samoa so much he moved here and is buried on a mountaintop overlooking the ocean.

SLB.letter: Halo! The Solomon Islands has some of the most biodiverse coral reefs left on Earth. During WWII, the famous Battle of Guadalcanal was fought here and you can still find rusting tanks and planes in the jungle. Some communities still use shell money for traditional ceremonies. The islands have over 70 languages for a population of about 700,000. Many islands are so remote that they are barely touched by the modern world. Kennedy Island is where JFK was stranded after his patrol boat was sunk by a Japanese destroyer.

VUT.letter: Halo! Vanuatu invented BUNGEE JUMPING. The original 'land diving' involves jumping from tall towers with vines tied to your ankles. Mount Yasur is one of the most accessible active volcanoes, and you can stand on the rim. Land diving inspired modern bungee jumping. But the original is WAY scarier because the vines are NOT elastic. You just... stop. Right before the ground.

TON.letter: Malo e lelei! Tonga is the only Pacific Island nation that was NEVER colonized by a foreign power. The king still has real authority. Humpback whales come here every year to have their babies, and you can actually swim with them in the warm water. Sunday is so sacred in Tonga that almost everything shuts down, even the airport. Tongans are famously generous, and feasts can include entire roasted pigs cooked in underground ovens. The Tongan rugby team regularly beats countries 100 times its size.

PLW.letter: Alii! Palau has a lake full of millions of golden jellyfish that do NOT sting. You can swim right through them. It is one of the most surreal experiences on Earth. Palau was the first country to create a national marine sanctuary covering 80% of its waters, and it banned reef-toxic sunscreen to protect its coral. When visitors arrive, they must sign a passport pledge promising to protect the environment. The Rock Islands are hundreds of mushroom-shaped limestone islands covered in jungle, scattered across turquoise water.

MHL.letter: Iakwe! The Marshall Islands are some of the most remote islands on Earth. Bikini Atoll is here, where nuclear bombs were tested. The Marshallese are master navigators who crossed the Pacific using only stars and wave patterns. Nuclear testing at Bikini Atoll was so powerful it vaporized entire islands. The crater is still visible from space. The bikini swimsuit was named after it because it was considered equally 'explosive.'

FSM.letter: Kaselehlie! The Federated States of Micronesia has Nan Madol, an ancient city built on artificial islands, sometimes called the 'Venice of the Pacific.' The stone money of Yap island is as tall as a person. The stone money of Yap is so heavy that nobody moves it. You just tell everyone you traded it. The rock stays where it is but you 'own' it.

NRU.letter: Anubumin! Nauru is the third smallest country in the world, a single tiny oval island in the middle of the Pacific. It was once one of the RICHEST countries per person on Earth because the entire island was made of phosphate (bird droppings accumulated over thousands of years that became valuable fertilizer). They mined it all and spent the money. Now the phosphate is almost gone and 90% of the island is a barren moonscape. Nauru is a warning about what happens when you use up everything and do not plan ahead.

KIR.letter: Mauri! Kiribati (say 'Kiri-bass') is the first country to see the sunrise each day. It's the only country in all four hemispheres (north, south, east, and west). Many islands are only a few feet above sea level. The government has already bought land in Fiji as a backup in case their islands go underwater from rising seas.

TUV.letter: Talofa! Tuvalu is the 4th smallest country in the world and might be the first to disappear. The highest point is only 15 feet above sea level. Rising oceans could swallow it entirely within decades. Tuvalu makes money in one of the cleverest ways any country has ever figured out: it sells its internet domain '.tv' to television companies around the world. The money from those two little letters is one of the country's biggest income sources. Only about 11,000 people live here across 9 tiny coral atolls.

XKX.letter: Përshëndetje! Kosovo is one of the newest countries in the world, declaring independence in 2008. Young people make up the majority of the population, making it one of Europe's youngest countries by age. Serbia says Kosovo is NOT a country. Over 100 other countries say it IS. The argument is still going. The Newborn monument in Pristina gets repainted every year on independence day. It started yellow and has been covered in flags, fingerprints, and murals.

BHR.letter: Marhaba! Bahrain is a tiny island kingdom in the Persian Gulf. It was the first place in the Arabian Peninsula to discover oil, and the first to know the oil would run out. So Bahrain built banks, hosted a Formula 1 race, and became a trading hub. The Tree of Life stands alone in the desert with no water source anyone can find. It has been alive for over 400 years.

GRL.letter: Aluu! Greenland is the world's biggest island and it belongs to Denmark, but it runs most of its own affairs. Despite the name, it is mostly covered in ICE. About 80% is a giant ice sheet. Only 56,000 people live here, which makes it one of the least crowded places on Earth. The Northern Lights dance across the sky in winter, and in summer the sun never sets.

facts.t: Sled dogs outnumber cars in some towns

HKG.letter: Nei hou! Hong Kong is one of the most incredible skylines on Earth. It was a British colony for over 150 years and returned to China in 1997. More skyscrapers here than anywhere in the world. The city runs on dim sum, double-decker trams, and the Star Ferry crossing Victoria Harbour. Seven million people packed onto islands and a tiny strip of mainland.

facts.t: 75% of the land is actually mountains and parks

PSE.letter: Marhaba! Palestine has some of the oldest continuously lived-in cities on Earth. Jericho may be over 10,000 years old. Olive trees here have been growing for thousands of years, and some families have been farming the same land for generations. The food is incredible: hummus, falafel, musakhan (roasted chicken on bread with sumac and onions). This land is at the center of one of the world's longest conflicts, and people here dream of peace.

ESH.letter: Ahlan! Western Sahara is one of the least populated places on Earth. It is mostly desert, and its status is disputed: Morocco controls most of it, but the Sahrawi people want independence. The Sahrawis are nomadic desert people with incredible survival skills. They make tea in the sand, navigate by the stars, and build entire camps that can move with them. The coastline is rich with fish, and the desert hides phosphate underground.

GUF.letter: Bonjour from South America! French Guiana is part of France, which means it uses euros, follows French law, and is technically part of the European Union even though it sits on the coast of South America. The European Space Agency launches rockets from here because it is close to the equator. Over 90% is covered in Amazon rainforest.

MYT.letter: Mayotte is a small French island that made a dramatic choice: when the other Comoros islands voted for independence from France, Mayotte said no thanks, we are staying French. That was in 1975 and the Comoros are still upset about it. The lagoon around Mayotte is one of the biggest enclosed lagoons in the world, a paradise for sea turtles who come to lay their eggs on the beaches. Lemurs live here too, the only place outside Madagascar where you can find them wild. Mayotte officially became a French department in 2011, which means it uses euros, follows French law, and sends representatives to Paris.

GLP.letter: Guadeloupe is a butterfly-shaped pair of islands in the Caribbean that belongs to France. Look at it on a map and you will see the wings. One island (Basse-Terre) has an active volcano called La Soufrière and thick rainforest so green it hurts your eyes. The other island (Grande-Terre) has white sand beaches and sugarcane fields. The food mixes French cooking with Caribbean spice, which means you can get a perfect croissant AND jerk chicken in the same town. Creole culture is everywhere. Guadeloupe uses euros, follows French law, and kids here learn the same curriculum as kids in Paris, but with palm trees outside the window.

MTQ.letter: Martinique is a French Caribbean island where you can eat a perfect croissant and then swim in turquoise water. Mount Pelée erupted in 1902 and destroyed the entire city of Saint-Pierre in minutes, one of the deadliest volcanic disasters ever. The island rebuilt and thrives on rum, bananas, and tourism. The 1902 eruption of Mount Pelée killed 30,000 people in minutes, wiping out the entire city of Saint-Pierre. One survivor was a prisoner in a thick-walled jail cell.

REU.letter: Réunion is a French island in the Indian Ocean near Madagascar with one of the most active volcanoes on Earth, Piton de la Fournaise. It erupts almost every year! The island has dramatic mountains, waterfalls, and some of the most diverse landscapes packed into a tiny space. Vanilla grows here and flavors ice cream around the world.

facts.t: One of Earth's most active volcanoes

PYF.letter: Ia orana! French Polynesia is 118 islands scattered across the South Pacific, including Tahiti and Bora Bora. The water is so clear you can see straight to the bottom. Paul Gauguin came here to paint and never wanted to leave. Polynesian navigation, tattoo culture, and black pearl farming are all born from these islands.

facts.t: Black pearl capital of the world

GUM.letter: Håfa Adai! Guam is a US territory in the western Pacific, closer to Tokyo than to Hawaii. The indigenous Chamorro people have lived here for over 4,000 years, making their culture one of the oldest in the Pacific. About a quarter of the island is US military property, which makes Guam one of the most strategically important dots on the American map. Guam is where America's day begins because it sits on the other side of the International Date Line. Like Puerto Rico, Guamanians are US citizens but cannot vote for president. The island survived brutal Japanese occupation during WWII, and Liberation Day (July 21) is the biggest holiday of the year.

ASM.letter: Talofa! American Samoa is a US territory in the South Pacific that produces more NFL players per capita than anywhere else on Earth. Football is practically a religion here. But here is the strange part: people born in American Samoa are NOT automatically US citizens. They are 'US nationals,' which means they can live and work in the US but cannot vote. People have been fighting in court to change this for years. The culture of fa'a Samoa (the Samoan way) is strong here with traditional chiefs, communal land, and family obligations that run deep. The National Park spans rainforest, coral reefs, and volcanic ridges.

MNP.letter: The Northern Mariana Islands are a US territory in the Pacific with the deepest point on Earth nearby, the Mariana Trench. Saipan is the main island. During WWII, some of the most intense battles of the Pacific were fought here. Today it is quiet, with beautiful beaches and Chamorro and Carolinian cultures. The Mariana Trench nearby is the deepest point on Earth at 36,000 feet. Mount Everest could fit inside it with a mile of water still above.

CUW.letter: Bon bini! Curaçao is a Dutch Caribbean island famous for its row of colorful buildings along the waterfront in Willemstad, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The famous blue Curaçao liqueur was invented here from local oranges. The island speaks Papiamentu, a Creole language mixing Portuguese, Spanish, Dutch, and African languages.

facts.t: Part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands

SXM.letter: Sint Maarten is the Dutch half of a tiny Caribbean island. The other half is French (Saint Martin). This makes it the smallest island in the world shared by two countries. The airport runway is so close to the beach that planes fly just over sunbathers' heads. One island, two countries, three languages! Maho Beach is where jet planes fly so low over sunbathers that the engine blast literally blows people off their feet. Tourists line up to experience it.

BES.letter: Bonaire is a tiny Dutch Caribbean island that is basically one giant marine park. The entire coastline is a protected reef, making it one of the best diving destinations on Earth. Pink flamingos wade in the salt flats. Bonaire is so serious about nature that every car license plate says 'Diver's Paradise.' Bonaire's license plates say 'Diver's Paradise.' The entire coastline is a protected marine park. You can walk into the water from shore and be surrounded by tropical fish in seconds.

facts.t: Entire coast is a marine park

AIA.letter: Anguilla is a tiny, flat British territory in the Caribbean with some of the most jaw-dropping beaches in the world. Shoal Bay is regularly voted the best beach in the Caribbean. The island runs on luxury tourism and has a surprisingly lively music scene with calypso, reggae, and soca. Only about 15,000 people live here. In 1967, Anguilla actually REVOLTED against being part of a federation with St. Kitts and Nevis. They kicked out the police, declared independence, and the British eventually sent in troops. The 'invasion' was so peaceful that the soldiers were greeted with cheers. Anguilla got what it wanted: to stay British, not to join St. Kitts.

facts.t: Shoal Bay: best beach in Caribbean

MSR.letter: Montserrat is called the 'Emerald Isle of the Caribbean' because of its Irish heritage. In 1995, the Soufrière Hills volcano erupted and buried the capital city of Plymouth under ash. It is the only modern capital city destroyed by a volcano. Half the island is still an exclusion zone. The people rebuilt on the other end.

facts.t: Half the island is exclusion zone

BLM.letter: Saint Barthélemy (Saint Barts) is a tiny French Caribbean island that is one of the most exclusive vacation spots on Earth. Celebrities and billionaires flock here for the pristine beaches and French cuisine. It was once Swedish! The harbor of Gustavia is named after a Swedish king. No building can be taller than a palm tree.

facts.t: No building taller than a palm tree

MAF.letter: Saint Martin is the French half of the same island as Sint Maarten. Legend says the French and Dutch divided it in 1648 by having a Frenchman and a Dutchman walk in opposite directions around the island. The French side is known for incredible restaurants. More cuisines per square mile than almost anywhere. Legend says the French and Dutch divided the island by having two men walk from opposite ends. The Frenchman walked farther because the Dutch guy stopped to drink. France got the bigger half.

facts.t: Divided by a legendary walk in 1648
No border fence between the two sides

SPM.letter: Saint Pierre and Miquelon is a tiny French territory just off the coast of Canada. It is the last piece of France in North America. Only about 6,000 people live here, and the culture is completely French: baguettes, euros, and the French flag, just a short ferry ride from Newfoundland. Fog rolls in constantly.

ATF.letter: The French Southern and Antarctic Lands are some of the most remote islands on Earth. No permanent population lives here, just scientists at research stations. King penguins, elephant seals, and albatrosses vastly outnumber humans. The Kerguelen Islands are called the 'Desolation Islands' because they are so isolated.

SJM.letter: Svalbard is a Norwegian archipelago in the Arctic Ocean where polar bears outnumber people. It is one of the northernmost inhabited places on Earth. The Global Seed Vault is buried in a mountain here, storing seeds from every country in case of global disaster. In winter, the sun does not rise for months. In summer, it never sets.

COK.letter: Kia orana! The Cook Islands are 15 tiny islands scattered across the South Pacific, linked to New Zealand in one of the most confusing political arrangements on Earth. Cook Islanders are New Zealand citizens, carry New Zealand passports, and can live in Auckland anytime. But the Cook Islands govern themselves, sign their own treaties, and make their own laws. Most countries do not recognize them as fully independent, but they act like it. Aitutaki's lagoon is considered one of the most beautiful in the world, a ring of turquoise water so clear you can see fish from the airplane. Rarotonga is the main island with volcanic mountains covered in jungle and a road that takes 30 minutes to drive all the way around.

NIU.letter: Kia orana! Niue is one of the smallest countries on Earth, a single raised coral island in the South Pacific. Only about 1,600 people live here. The entire country is basically one village where everyone knows everyone. Humpback whales swim right up to the shore and you can watch them from the cliffs. Niue was the first country to offer free nationwide WiFi, which is wild for a place this remote. More Niueans live in New Zealand (about 30,000) than on Niue itself. The island is made of ancient coral reef pushed up from the ocean floor, full of caves and secret swimming holes.

PCN.letter: Pitcairn is the most remote inhabited island on Earth. Only about 50 people live here, ALL descended from the mutineers of HMS Bounty who arrived in 1790 after one of the most famous mutinies in history. There is no airport and no harbor. A ship visits a few times a year. To get mail, they have to wait for a passing vessel. The island is a British territory and everyone knows everyone because there are literally 50 of them. Pitcairn honey is considered some of the purest in the world because the island is so isolated that the bees have never been exposed to disease.

WLF.letter: Wallis and Futuna is a French territory in the South Pacific with three traditional kingdoms that still have real kings. It is one of the few places where a European government and Polynesian monarchies coexist. The islands are tiny and remote. Kava ceremonies and Catholic churches sit side by side. The three traditional kings attend French state functions alongside elected officials. It is one of the last places where Polynesian royalty and European governance genuinely coexist.

CYN.letter: Northern Cyprus is the Turkish-speaking northern part of the island of Cyprus. After a conflict in 1974, the island was divided. Only Turkey recognizes Northern Cyprus as a country. The capital Nicosia is the last divided capital in the world, with a buffer zone running through its center. Ancient ruins and quiet beaches dot the coast.

ATA.letter: Antarctica is the coldest, driest, windiest continent on Earth. Nobody lives here permanently, but scientists from 30 countries work at research stations studying ice, weather, and penguins. The ice sheet holds 70% of all the fresh water on Earth. If it melted, sea levels would rise 200 feet. Emperor penguins march across the ice in temperatures of -60°F.
